title: The Curse of the Missing Wi-Fi
subtitle: A PyConDE 2026 Interlude, Chapter I
narrator_intro: Something stirs beneath the conference hall. Five questions. One mystery. Let the scrying pool reveal what happened below.

rounds.poll_question: What's the best Python library for data science?
What's the most overrated tech buzzword?
What do you shout when your code finally compiles?
Your go-to excuse for missing a deadline?

story_template: It happened without warning: the Wi-Fi died. A collective gasp rippled through the conference hall. Valerio the Chance Caster, mid-roll of his enchanted dice, froze. 'This is no ordinary outage,' he whispered. 'Someone has unleashed a {CREATURE} upon the router.'

Johannes the Timekeeper checked his watch, checked it again, and announced with clockwork precision: 'We have exactly twelve minutes before the next talk. Someone must go below.' He thrust a glowing {MAGIC_TOOL} into the nearest volunteer's hands. 'Take this. You will need it.'

Down in the server dungeon, the air hummed with cursed packets. The volunteer found the source: a tangle of corrupted cables and a glowing rune that pulsed with eldritch light. Following an ancient sysadmin ritual, they typed '{INCANTATION}' into the terminal and held their breath.

Lisa, Leader of Team Other, appeared from the shadows. 'Ah yes,' she said, examining the blinking lights with a knowing look. 'I have seen this before. The router needs {SECRET_INGREDIENT}. Do not ask how I know. I lead... the other team.' She smiled mysteriously and vanished.

With trembling hands, the volunteer applied the fix, leaned close to the dying router, and whispered: '{VICTORY_PHRASE}'. A hum. A flicker. The Wi-Fi returned. Five hundred developers reconnected simultaneously and the internet groaned under the weight of their Stack Overflow tabs.

But as the lights steadied, a new message blinked across every screen in the building: 'THE SLIDES WERE NEVER YOURS. THE QUEST BEGINS TOMORROW.' Valerio the Chance Caster looked up from his dice and murmured: 'Well... that is not good.'
